headline: Tax Revenue Per Capita at RM7,400 — Lowest in Developed ASEAN
context: Malaysia's tax revenue per capita of RM7,400 in 2025 ranked lowest among upper-middle-income ASEAN nations, below Thailand's RM8,900 and well behind Singapore's RM42,000, reflecting a narrow tax base and widespread non-compliance.

cards.big: RM7,400 tax per capita — Malaysia collects less per person than Thailand despite 28% higher GDP per capita
cards.sub: Only 4.2 million Malaysians out of 16 million workforce actually pay income tax — a 26% taxpayer rate

cards.big: Tax-to-GDP ratio at 11.8% is structurally insufficient to fund a middle-income nation's public service needs
cards.sub: Closing the gap to Thailand's 16.5% would generate an additional RM76 billion — eliminating the fiscal deficit entirely

cards.big: Informal economy estimated at 21% of GDP — RM382 billion in economic activity generates near-zero tax contribution
cards.sub: Night market traders, freelancers, and cash-based businesses operate entirely outside the tax system — by necessity, not evasion

cards.big: LHDN workforce of 12,800 manages 28 million tax files — Singapore's IRAS manages 4 million files with 2,100 staff at 4x productivity
cards.sub: Digitalisation of tax filing reached 78% but audit and compliance functions remain largely manual and paper-based

cards.big: A country that wants first-world services but collects developing-world taxes has made a choice — it just hasn't told its citizens yet
Should Malaysia implement a comprehensive tax reform package combining 4% GST with lower income tax rates and expanded B40 exemptions?
